sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: Students built a copper concentration cell using Cu(s) electrodes dipped into Cu²⁺ solutions of different concentrations. One half-cell contained 1.0 M Cu²⁺, while the other varied from 1.0 M to 0.001 M Cu²⁺. The measured cell potentials were approximately 0.000 V when both were 1.0 M, 0.030 V for a 10-fold difference, 0.059 V for a 100-fold difference, and 0.089 V for a 1000-fold difference at 25°C.
questions.stem: What cell potential is measured for a 100-fold concentration difference?

explanations.C: Correct. The figure shows about 0.059 V for a 100-fold difference in Cu²⁺ concentration. This is the expected Nernst behavior for a two-electron copper cell.
explanations.A: 0.000 V occurs when the concentrations are equal.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Cell potential of a copper concentration cell increases with concentration ratio.

questions.stem: Why is the cell potential zero when both Cu²⁺ concentrations are 1.0 M?

choices.B: There is no concentration gradient to drive net electron flow
choices.C: The electrodes are made of different metals
choices.D: The salt bridge blocks ion movement

explanations.B: Correct. A concentration cell depends on a difference in ion concentration. When both half-cells are identical, there is no thermodynamic driving force and Ecell = 0.
explanations.A: Copper metal is conductive and serves as the electrode.
explanations.C: The electrodes are both copper in a concentration cell.
explanations.D: A salt bridge permits ion movement to maintain charge balance.

questions.stem: In the dilute half-cell, which process tends to occur?

choices.D: Oxidation of Cu(s) to increase Cu²⁺ concentration
choices.A: Reduction of Cu²⁺ to make the dilute side more dilute
choices.B: No half-reaction can occur in a concentration cell
choices.C: Reduction of water is the required cathode reaction

explanations.D: Correct. The dilute side acts as the anode: Cu(s) is oxidized to Cu²⁺, increasing the dilute ion concentration and reducing the concentration difference.
explanations.A: Reduction would consume Cu²⁺ and worsen the concentration difference on the dilute side.
explanations.B: Concentration cells still use oxidation and reduction half-reactions.
explanations.C: The passage describes a copper concentration cell, not water electrolysis.

questions.stem: Which equation is most directly used to predict the concentration dependence of Ecell?

explanations.A: Correct. The Nernst equation relates cell potential to reaction quotient and ion concentrations. It explains the logarithmic concentration dependence shown.
explanations.B: Henderson-Hasselbalch describes buffer pH, not electrochemical potential.
explanations.C: Snell’s law describes refraction of light.
explanations.D: Poiseuille’s law describes laminar fluid flow in tubes.

questions.stem: Why does the potential increase less than linearly as the concentration ratio grows?

choices.D: The Nernst dependence on concentration ratio is logarithmic
choices.A: The voltage is proportional to the square of concentration ratio
choices.B: The cell runs out of copper immediately
choices.C: Cell potential is independent of concentration ratio

explanations.D: Correct. Nernst behavior gives E proportional to log of the concentration ratio. Each 10-fold change adds a similar voltage increment rather than multiplying voltage linearly.
explanations.A: A squared relationship would make the voltage increase much more steeply than shown.
explanations.B: The passage reports measured potentials across ratios, so the cell is not described as immediately exhausted.
explanations.C: The graph directly shows dependence on concentration ratio.

questions.passage: An electrolysis lab plated nickel metal from Ni²⁺ solution onto an electrode. Current was held constant at 2.0 A. The measured mass of nickel deposited after 5, 10, and 20 minutes was approximately 0.18 g, 0.36 g, and 0.73 g. Students used Faraday’s law and the half-reaction Ni²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Ni(s). Faraday’s constant was approximated as 96,500 C/mol e⁻.
questions.stem: What mass of nickel was deposited after 10 minutes?

explanations.C: Correct. Figure 2 shows about 0.36 g deposited after 10 minutes. The linear trend reflects constant current.
explanations.D: 2.0 is the current in amperes, not the plated mass.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Mass of nickel deposited increases approximately linearly with electrolysis time at constant current.

questions.stem: Why is mass deposited approximately proportional to time?

choices.B: At constant current, total charge passed is proportional to time
choices.A: The molar mass of nickel increases with time
choices.D: Electrons are not involved in electroplating

explanations.B: Correct. Charge is Q = It. If current is constant, longer time means proportionally more charge, electrons, and plated nickel.
explanations.A: Molar mass is a fixed property and does not increase during the trial.
explanations.C: Faraday’s constant is a constant, not a time-dependent value.
explanations.D: Electrons are required to reduce Ni²⁺ to Ni metal.

questions.stem: How many moles of electrons are required to deposit 1 mol of Ni(s)?

explanations.A: Correct. The half-reaction Ni²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Ni(s) shows that two moles of electrons are required per mole of nickel deposited.
explanations.B: One electron would reduce Ni²⁺ only to Ni⁺, not Ni(s).
explanations.C: Half a mole of electrons is not consistent with the balanced half-reaction.
explanations.D: Four electrons is twice the required electron stoichiometry.

questions.stem: What charge passes through the cell in 10 minutes at 2.0 A?

explanations.C: Correct. Q = It = 2.0 C/s × 600 s = 1200 C. The time must be converted from minutes to seconds.
explanations.A: 20 C uses minutes incorrectly without converting to seconds.
explanations.B: 600 C uses 1.0 A instead of 2.0 A.
explanations.D: 12,000 C is too large by a factor of 10.

questions.stem: If current were doubled for the same time, what would happen to deposited nickel mass?

explanations.B: Correct. Faraday’s law makes deposited mass proportional to charge, and charge is proportional to current. Doubling current at the same time doubles charge and therefore doubles deposited mass.
explanations.A: Halving would require lower charge, not higher current.
explanations.C: Current directly affects charge passed, so mass would not remain unchanged.
explanations.D: Quadrupling would require both current and time to double or another squared dependence, which is not present.
